title: Indexes & Performance
description: Create indexes, read query plans with explain(), and understand when indexes help vs hurt.

sections.content: Without an index, MongoDB performs a **collection scan** — reading every document to find matches. This is exactly like a PostgreSQL sequential scan (`Seq Scan`). As your collection grows, this gets painfully slow.

sections.heading: See the problem — no index
Single-field and compound indexes

sections.content: **ESR Rule for compound indexes:** Order fields as **Equality → Sort → Range**. Equality filters first, then sort fields, then range filters. This matches how MongoDB uses the index internally.

sections.heading: Text indexes — full text search
Understanding explain() output

sections.content: **Index overhead:** Every index speeds up reads but slows down writes (insert/update/delete must update all indexes). Don't index every field — focus on fields used in frequent, slow queries. Same trade-off exists in PostgreSQL.

**totalKeysExamined vs totalDocsExamined.** In explain() output, `totalKeysExamined` is how many index entries were scanned. `totalDocsExamined` is how many full documents were loaded from disk. When you use an index (IXSCAN), MongoDB first scans index keys, then fetches only the matching documents. A covered query (see below) has `totalDocsExamined: 0` because all needed data comes from the index itself.

**Why the query planner might ignore your index.** MongoDB's query planner compares candidate plans and picks the cheapest one. If a field has very low cardinality (e.g. `inStock` is `true` for 80% of documents), the planner may decide a full collection scan is faster than using the index — because following index pointers to 80% of all documents has more overhead than just reading all documents sequentially.

**A covered query never reads documents — it reads only the index.** This is the fastest possible query. A query is covered when every field it needs (both in the filter and in the projection) is present in the index, and `_id` is excluded from the projection.

sections.heading: Covered query example
Covered query
table: Index-only scan (when all columns are in the index)

sections.content: **Partial indexes** index only the documents that match a filter expression. This creates a smaller, more targeted index compared to indexing the entire collection.

sections.heading: Partial index — only index active orders

sections.content: **createIndex() is idempotent.** Calling it multiple times with the same definition is safe — MongoDB detects the duplicate and does nothing. You will not create two identical indexes.

**Index intersection.** MongoDB can combine two separate single-field indexes to satisfy a single query. For example, a query on both `authorId` and `price` could use an index on `authorId` AND an index on `price` together. However, a well-designed compound index almost always outperforms index intersection — so prefer compound indexes over relying on this.
